title: When War Becomes a Script and Fame Becomes a Trap
subtitle: Sneako interviews Professor Jiang after the Iran war predictions go viral
voice_policy: Direct argumentative prose derived from the interview. Preserve Sneako's pressure where it shapes Jiang's answers, while keeping disputed claims as dated source claims rather than external verification.
trace_policy: Paragraph-level transcript refs with selective evidence marks on sharp thesis phrases, dated predictions, and jump-worthy moments.
dek: Sneako presses Jiang after the Iran war turns him into a sudden internet figure. Jiang answers with a warning: war does not merely kill abroad. It scripts civil discord, rewards religious extremism, turns media personalities into controlled assets, and tempts the teacher to become a player in the very game he is trying to explain.
thesis: The interview is not just about whether Jiang predicted the Iran war. It is about what prediction does to the person who becomes useful to the crowd. Jiang reads the war as an eschatological and imperial script: Christian Zionism, Al-Aqsa, the Third Temple, Trump's survival politics, draft pressure, Gulf fragility, and Greater Israel all move through the same logic of escalation. But he also turns the lens back on himself and on Sneako: fame is a drug, monetization is an invitation to control, education is self-annihilation of brainwashing, and the best survival asset is not crypto or platform reach but human relationships.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: The strange force of this interview is that the war and the influencer economy become one problem. Sneako wants to know why the Iran war is escalating, why anti-war voices are being attacked, why protests are absent, and why people who should agree are turning on each other. Jiang keeps answering at two scales at once. At the geopolitical scale, war is a script that needs civil discord, religious acceleration, and imperial overreach. At the personal scale, sudden fame is also a script: it offers money, status, enemies, fans, and a role to play. Jiang's answer is to keep the teacher separate from the player.

marks.text: war is a script that needs civil discord
sudden fame is also a script
keep the teacher separate from the player

beats.heading: The Teacher In The Storm
beats.summary: The interview starts with attacks on Jiang, his March anxiety, and the question of whether sudden celebrity would damage his ability to teach.

paragraphs.text: The opening is already unstable. Before the live greeting, the video frames Jiang as someone who predicted the Iran war, was attacked for credentials and jokes, and had not slept for two weeks when the war began. Sneako then asks about the attack campaign, and Jiang refuses to let the credential scandal become the center. In the middle of a war, the question of whether he is a credentialed professor is a distraction.

marks.text: the credential scandal becomes the center

paragraphs.text: Jiang names the three attacks directly: he is not a real professor, he is an antisemite or Holocaust denier, or he is somehow a spy for every side at once. But the real pressure was not reputation. It was the war. He says the February 28 outbreak left him sleepless, anxious for his children, and unable to stop following the news.

marks.text: somehow a spy for every side at once
sleepless, anxious for his children

paragraphs.text: The deeper question is what fame does to judgment. Jiang says March put him in the eye of the storm and made him ask whether he could still do insightful analysis while becoming a celebrity. If fame damages the analysis, the correct move is to stop being a celebrity and return to teaching and research. His stated mission is not to become a media player: raise his children, educate people who want education, and keep learning with curiosity and objectivity.

marks.text: could still do insightful analysis while becoming a celebrity
raise his children, educate people, and keep learning

beats.heading: War Makes People Crazy
beats.summary: Sneako asks whether escalation can still be avoided; Jiang says a deal is possible in theory, but war produces its own adrenaline and loss-of-face logic.

paragraphs.text: Sneako pushes the immediate strategic question: why not negotiate with Iran, reopen Hormuz, and avoid ground invasion? Jiang says the deal exists. If the United States stopped the war, closed Middle East bases, and offered a win-win arrangement around navigation, investment, and dollar-system participation, Iran would likely negotiate. This is the absurdity: the art of the deal is available, but the war path is chosen.

marks.text: the deal exists
the art of the deal is available, but the war path is chosen

paragraphs.text: Jiang then gives the mechanism: war makes people crazy. Once actors are inside the fight, adrenaline and humiliation take over. Hegseth appears to love the bomb language; Trump fears being called a clown or incompetent. The result is escalation for face. Bomb power plants, send ground troops, normalize madness, and keep going until total war feels ordinary.

marks.text: war makes people crazy
escalation for face

paragraphs.text: Sneako adds the media image: war is marketed like an 1980s action movie or a video game. Drone footage becomes an Xbox controller view. Jiang does not treat that as aesthetic trivia. The problem is that distance, spectacle, and repeated crisis lower the threshold for total war.

marks.text: war is marketed like an action movie or video game

beats.heading: Eschatology As Political Engine
beats.summary: The Christian Zionism and Al-Aqsa discussion turns religious belief into a material political engine: money, lobbying, red heifers, temple preparation, and crisis timing.

paragraphs.text: Asked about evangelical rhetoric, Jiang returns to an older prediction: a U.S. invasion of Iran would be driven by Christian Zionism, Saudi Arabia, and imperial hubris. He emphasizes the first more than many viewers expect. For Christian Zionists, Middle East war is not simply policy. It is a way to trigger prophecy: war, Al-Aqsa, Third Temple, Jewish return, Gog and Magog, Second Coming.

marks.text: Christian Zionism, Saudi Arabia, and imperial hubris
a way to trigger prophecy

paragraphs.text: The strongest formulation is that eschatology behaves like a script. It gives ordinary people a role, a line, a purpose, and the feeling that history will move almost divinely if they do their small part. That is why the movement is powerful: belief becomes fundraising, lobbying, settlement money, and daily pressure on politicians. AIPAC money and Christian Zionist discipline fuse into an American political force.

marks.text: eschatology behaves like a script
belief becomes fundraising, lobbying, settlement money, and daily pressure

paragraphs.text: Sneako then asks why Al-Aqsa was closed and whether blame could be pinned on Iran. Jiang answers with a disturbing inventory: red heifers bred in Texas, the Temple Institute, excavations under the complex, and a possible controlled-demolition false flag during global chaos. The point is not to verify every event from outside the source. The point is how Jiang reads religious preparation, crisis timing, and imperial war as one machine.

marks.text: red heifers, the Temple Institute, excavations under the complex
religious preparation, crisis timing, and imperial war as one machine

beats.heading: Social Media As Civil-War Training
beats.summary: Instead of censorship, Jiang suspects selective amplification: let anti-war voices speak, then turn allies into enemies and despair into civil discord.

paragraphs.text: Jiang compares this moment to COVID and sees an inversion. During COVID, dissent was censored. In this war, prominent anti-war figures are amplified. That does not reassure him. If the war is meant to produce civil discord, then letting people speak can also be a way to make them fight, split, and despair.

marks.text: prominent anti-war figures are amplified
letting people speak can also be a way to make them fight

paragraphs.text: Sneako recognizes the same mechanism inside his own world: AI accounts, racial attacks, faith attacks, Muslims turning against Muslims, and former allies attacking Jiang. Jiang answers by separating himself from partisan influencer logic. He is not telling people to vote for Trump and wait for salvation. He sees himself as a teacher trying to help people think for themselves.

marks.text: not partisan, but a teacher

paragraphs.text: The DEI discussion gives the class version of the same model. Occupy Wall Street frightened the powerful because it united people against the banking elite. DEI and woke politics then redirected class anger into race, gender, and historical grievance. The trick worked so well that young people became hard to reason with, but it also created a new problem: the same moral machinery could turn against Gaza.

marks.text: redirected class anger into race, gender, and historical grievance
the same moral machinery could turn against Gaza

paragraphs.text: When Sneako asks how billions can be controlled by so few, Jiang names the intellectual class. The 1 percent hold money and command, but the anxious 10 percent curate opinion: journalists, teachers, professors, doctors, credentialed explainers. COVID taught them obedience programming. AI now threatens to replace them. If they cannot control public opinion, Jiang says, they are worthless to the power elite.

marks.text: the anxious 10 percent curate opinion
If they cannot control public opinion, they are worthless

beats.heading: Trump As Projection And Survival Machine
beats.summary: Jiang reads Trump through managed succession, projection, third-term survival, and the military optics culture that turns war into spectacle.

paragraphs.text: Jiang’s Clinton-Bush story is a model of political succession as managed transformation. He argues that Clinton could do what Bush could not: pass NAFTA, open China trade, and turn the Democratic Party from a working-class party into the coastal professional elite party. That frame prepares the Trump claim: Biden could not launch the Iran war without MAGA revolt, but Trump can.

marks.text: Biden could not launch the Iran war, but Trump can

paragraphs.text: Trump’s incoherence is not only a flaw. It is a projection surface. Because he contradicts himself constantly, supporters can imagine a hidden 5D chess plan: use the war to destroy the deep state, become a monarch, and restore democracy by emergency. Jiang treats that as fantasy, but fantasy with political force.

marks.text: Trump’s incoherence is a projection surface
become a monarch, and restore democracy by emergency

paragraphs.text: The third-term prediction follows from survival. Jiang says Trump will not leave office because leaving means Democratic prosecution of him and his family. The mechanism could be emergency powers, ICE as secret police, National Guard deployment, a false flag, a vice-presidential route through Don Jr., or a friendly Supreme Court. The details are uncertain; the survival incentive is the structure.

marks.text: The details are uncertain; the survival incentive is the structure.

paragraphs.text: Then the military story becomes theater. Jiang says a downed F-15 rescue already functions as a ground incursion and sets precedent. American jets and air war are not built for a long campaign. Worse, special-operations culture turns war into optics: the soldier is thinking about the book deal, the movie deal, the heroic rescue scene. Jessica Lynch becomes the historical template for spectacle replacing strategy.

marks.text: a ground incursion and sets precedent
spectacle replacing strategy

beats.heading: Greater Israel And The Cost Of Redemption
beats.summary: The conversation moves from draft pressure and sabotage into Kabbalah, Greater Israel, Gulf collapse, and the claim that sacred redemption is being pursued through regional destruction.

paragraphs.text: Jiang says war has a logical momentum of its own: if the United States starts bombing Iran, it then needs ground forces to solve the air-defense problems produced by the bombing. Sneako names this as sunken cost. Jiang extends it to draft pressure, rationing, National Guard control, German draft movement, low morale, and even fragging or sabotage if young men are sent to die in the Middle East.

marks.text: war has a logical momentum of its own
fragging or sabotage

paragraphs.text: When the exchange returns to Israel, Jiang gives his harshest theological reading. He says racial supremacy in Israel is real and gives a Kabbalah-inflected account of divine roots, animal souls, and mowing the lawn. The read should not launder this into neutrality: it is Jiang’s dated, provocative claim about how theology can become dehumanization.

marks.text: theology can become dehumanization
mowing the lawn

paragraphs.text: Asked about the Wailing Wall and sacred sexuality, Jiang says he is speculating, not speaking as a rabbi. His useful model is will to bestow and will to receive: God gives, humanity cannot receive without ego and embarrassment, and spiritual life becomes a process of reducing ego so reunion with God can happen. The banquet allegory is the memorable image: the host prepares a feast, the guest panics and runs away.

marks.text: will to bestow and will to receive
the host prepares a feast, the guest panics and runs away

paragraphs.text: Sneako then asks when Greater Israel appears. Jiang answers with Netanyahu, Hamas, October 7, Qatar funding, and a wider claim: Greater Israel and the Third Temple are not Netanyahu’s private project but a redemptive history project. Iran, the GCC, Turkey, and the U.S. retreat become dominoes. The war ends, in his telling, when America is forced out of the Middle East and Israel can move.

marks.text: a redemptive history project
when America is forced out of the Middle East

paragraphs.text: The Gulf states are not treated as solid powers. Jiang calls them mirages: welfare monarchies and caste societies where expatriates do the work and poorer migrants are treated as slaves. If the war ruins the Gulf image, the fantasy land in the desert stops looking inevitable.

marks.text: mirages
fantasy land in the desert

beats.heading: Money, Fame, And Real Investment
beats.summary: The final movement turns from world collapse to personal discipline: do not monetize yourself into capture, do not feed trolls, and invest in relationships.

paragraphs.text: Jordan Peterson becomes the cautionary double. Jiang praises the early teacher, then says monetization captured him. Money is how control enters. Jiang monetizes Substack but refuses YouTube monetization as a signal: do not come buy me. The devil can only come in if invited.

marks.text: Money is how control enters
The devil can only come in if invited

paragraphs.text: Asked what future generations should invest in if empire collapses, Jiang does not say Bitcoin. He says human relationships: cook dinner together, hike together, be generous, build friendships, emotional bonds, and communities that could grow food together if the world forces people out of cities. Survival capital is trust.

marks.text: Survival capital is trust

paragraphs.text: The same discipline governs online attention. Jiang says education is self-annihilation of brainwashing, but social media is re-brainwashing by reaction. His rule is not to respond online. Have long-form conversations if needed, but do not become a keyboard warrior. The troll is better at trolling than the teacher is.

marks.text: education is self-annihilation of brainwashing
do not become a keyboard warrior

paragraphs.text: He has learned this because he has been famous before. In his early China education fame he Googled himself every day, felt the rush, and learned that fame is a drug. If today gets 10,000 views and tomorrow gets 1,000, self-worth collapses into metrics. That is why the teacher must protect the conditions of teaching.

marks.text: fame is a drug
self-worth collapses into metrics

paragraphs.text: The closing advice to Sneako is therefore not a growth hack. Move offline. Do more in-person conversations. Bring difficult people together: David Icke, Alexander Dugin, Marandi, George Galloway. The medium matters because a real conversation can resist the online machinery that turns every disagreement into performance.

marks.text: Move offline
a real conversation can resist the online machinery

questions.question: Has the sudden rise, the attacks, and the pressure made this overwhelming for you personally?
questions.answer: Jiang says March was difficult because the Iran war made him sleepless and anxious for his children; the smear campaign bothered him less than the danger of where the war could lead.

questions.question: Do you still think this war is the decline or end of the American empire?
questions.answer: Jiang says yes: war makes people crazy, creates adrenaline and loss-of-face escalation, and pushes the U.S. toward bombing, ground troops, and total war.

questions.question: What is the truth about evangelical Christians and this war rhetoric?
questions.answer: Jiang says Christian Zionism is a major driver: believers read Middle East war, Al-Aqsa, the Third Temple, Jewish return, and the Second Coming as a prophetic sequence they can help accelerate.

questions.question: Why was Al-Aqsa closed, and is there a reason connected to a false-flag scenario?
questions.answer: Jiang answers with a speculative playbook: red heifers, the Temple Institute, digs under the complex, and a possible demolition timed for wider global chaos.

questions.question: Have they shifted from censorship to demoralization and propaganda saturation?
questions.answer: Jiang says the current pattern differs from COVID: instead of suppressing dissent, anti-war influencers are being amplified, which he suspects may serve a civil-discord script.

questions.question: How can almost eight billion people be controlled by such a small few?
questions.answer: Jiang says the controlling class is less the top 1 percent than the anxious intellectual 10 percent that curates opinion, fears populist revolt and AI replacement, and therefore works to manage discourse.

questions.question: How could Trump get a third term, and is it because of wartime?
questions.answer: Jiang predicts Trump will not leave office because leaving invites prosecution; possible mechanisms include emergency powers, ICE and National Guard expansion, false flags, a VP route, and a friendly Supreme Court.

questions.question: When could a draft happen, and would it catalyze protest?
questions.answer: Jiang points to Germany’s restrictions on military-age men and predicts draft planning in Germany and America, but warns morale may be so low that sabotage and fragging become risks.

questions.question: When can we see Greater Israel?
questions.answer: Jiang says Greater Israel is tied to the Third Temple and redemptive history; the Iran war weakens regional obstacles until U.S. retreat from the Middle East clears the path.

questions.question: What should future generations invest in if the American empire collapses?
questions.answer: Jiang says young people should invest in human relationships: cook, hike, be generous, build friendships, and form communities that can survive together if cities and systems fail.

source_notes: This interview is dated April 6, 2026 by YouTube metadata fallback. It contains many current-event claims and predictions about Iran, Israel, Trump, the draft, and the Gulf; the public read preserves them as Jiang’s dated claims, not as external verification.

The transcript has ASR noise in proper names and several host fragments, especially around Hegseth, Al-Aqsa, Hormuz, Iblis/Gabriel, and some late outro material. The read cleans only where the speaker’s meaning is clear and keeps disputed material attributed.
